description: Conversion of raw materials into finished goods on machines/lines across shifts. Levers are asset effectiveness (OEE), quality (yield, scrap), flow (cycle/takt time, throughput), reliability (downtime, MTBF/MTTR), and schedule adherence (OTIF).

metrics.name: Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)
metrics.definition: Single score for how productively a machine ran: the PRODUCT of three rates — Availability x Performance x Quality — each a ratio in 0..1.
metrics.formula: OEE = Availability * Performance * Quality, computed PER machine PER shift, where Availability = run_time / planned_production_time, Performance = (ideal_cycle_time * total_units) / run_time, Quality = good_units / total_units. Aggregate by run-time-weighting the per-machine-shift OEE, NOT by averaging the three rates.
metrics.grain: Machine x shift grain. Compute each of the three sub-rates per machine per shift, MULTIPLY them, then roll up. Each sub-rate is bounded 0..1, so OEE is bounded 0..1 by construction.
anti_patterns: Averaging Availability, Performance, and Quality together (e.g. (A+P+Q)/3) — OEE is their PRODUCT, not their mean.
AVG(oee) across machines unweighted — a machine that ran 8h and one that ran 10min count equally; weight by run_time (or planned_production_time).
Computing the three rates over different windows/grains and multiplying mismatched values.
Any sub-rate or final OEE > 1 is a bug: run_time can't exceed planned time, output can't exceed ideal-rate capacity, good can't exceed total.
metrics.sane_range: ratio 0..1 (world-class ~0.85; >1 is impossible — a grain/window error).

metrics.name: First Pass Yield (FPY) / Quality Rate
metrics.definition: Share of units that pass inspection on the first attempt without rework or scrap.
metrics.formula: good_units / NULLIF(total_units_started, 0), where good_units exclude anything reworked or scrapped.
metrics.grain: Per line/product/shift then aggregate. Denominator = units STARTED (or inspected), not just units that reached the end. Sum good and total at the same grain before dividing.
anti_patterns: Counting reworked units as 'good' — FPY means right the FIRST time; rework belongs in the fail bucket.
AVG(per_row_yield_pct) instead of SUM(good)/SUM(total) — unweighted average distorts when batch sizes differ.
Denominator = units finished rather than units started hides scrap that left the line early.

metrics.name: Scrap / Defect Rate (PPM)
metrics.definition: Fraction of produced/inspected units that are defective, often expressed in parts per million.
metrics.grain: Per product/line/period. Denominator MUST be units produced or inspected over the same window as the defect count — never total orders, total rows after a join, or a different period.
anti_patterns: Denominator = number of production runs or joined rows instead of units produced — fans out and meaningless.
Counting defect records (one defective unit can have several defect codes) instead of distinct defective units inflates the numerator.
Reporting the raw ratio as if it were PPM (off by 1,000,000x) or vice versa.
defect_rate > 1 is a bug — more defects than units means double-counted defects or wrong denominator.

metrics.name: Cycle Time vs Takt Time
metrics.definition: Cycle time = actual time to produce one unit; takt time = customer-demand pace (available time per unit required). Comparing them shows if the line keeps up with demand.
metrics.formula: actual_cycle_time = run_time / NULLIF(total_units, 0); takt_time = available_production_time / NULLIF(customer_demand_units, 0). Line keeps pace iff actual_cycle_time <= takt_time.
metrics.grain: Per line/station/period. Use the SAME time unit (seconds or minutes) for both. Cycle time is run_time divided by units made; takt is available time divided by units DEMANDED — different denominators, do not conflate.
anti_patterns: Dividing total time by number of production ORDERS instead of units yields per-order time, not per-unit cycle time.
Mixing units (seconds vs minutes) between cycle and takt makes the comparison nonsense.
Using calendar time instead of available/planned time for takt overstates capacity.
metrics.sane_range: time per unit, strictly positive (seconds to minutes for discrete parts); cycle <= takt means demand is met.

metrics.name: Throughput (Units per Period)
metrics.definition: Good units produced per unit of time over a defined window.
metrics.formula: SUM(good_units) / time_window, e.g. SUM(good_units) per hour = SUM(good_units) / (run_time_hours).
metrics.grain: Per line/machine over an explicit time window. ALWAYS state the window (per hour, per shift, per day) — a count with no time basis is not throughput. Sum units over the window, divide by the window length.
anti_patterns: Reporting a raw unit count with no time window — that's volume, not a rate.
Counting units from a fanned-out join (units multiplied by defect rows or shift rows) overstates output.
Including scrapped units in throughput when the metric is GOOD output — be explicit about good vs total.
metrics.sane_range: units per period, non-negative; bounded above by ideal-rate capacity for the window.

metrics.name: Unplanned Downtime & MTBF / MTTR
metrics.definition: Downtime = time a machine was unexpectedly stopped during scheduled time; MTBF = mean operating time between failures; MTTR = mean time to repair a failure.
metrics.formula: unplanned_downtime = SUM(downtime_event_duration) over the window; MTBF = total_operating_time / NULLIF(COUNT(failure_events), 0); MTTR = SUM(repair_duration) / NULLIF(COUNT(repair_events), 0).
metrics.grain: Per machine over a calendar window. Downtime must be SUMMED over the SAME window as scheduled/operating time used elsewhere (e.g. OEE availability) — misaligned windows corrupt availability. Count distinct failure events, not log rows.
anti_patterns: Summing downtime over a different calendar window than scheduled time, so downtime + uptime != scheduled time.
Counting overlapping or duplicate event rows as separate failures inflates the event count and deflates MTBF.
Mixing planned maintenance into UNPLANNED downtime overstates the loss.
Dividing by zero failures (a perfect machine) must yield NULL/undefined MTBF, not 0.
metrics.sane_range: downtime in minutes/hours <= scheduled time; MTBF/MTTR positive durations (MTBF >> MTTR for healthy assets).

metrics.name: Capacity Utilization
metrics.definition: Share of available/theoretical capacity that was actually used to produce.
metrics.formula: actual_output / NULLIF(theoretical_max_output, 0)  -- or used_machine_time / available_machine_time, over the same window.
metrics.grain: Per machine/line/plant over a defined window. Numerator and denominator must use the SAME basis (both in units, or both in machine-hours) and the same window. Roll up by summing capacities, not averaging utilization percentages.
anti_patterns: Comparing actual units to a capacity measured over a different time span.
AVG(utilization_pct) across machines of different sizes — weight by capacity (or machine-hours) instead.
Utilization > 1 signals capacity understated or output double-counted (a join fan-out), not genuine overproduction.
metrics.sane_range: ratio 0..1 (commonly 0.6–0.9; brief >1 only from overtime above nameplate, otherwise a bug).
needs_concepts: actual output or used machine time

metrics.name: On-Time-In-Full (OTIF) / Schedule Adherence
metrics.definition: Share of orders (or scheduled production jobs) delivered/completed ON time AND IN FULL — both conditions must hold.
metrics.formula: COUNT(DISTINCT order_id WHERE delivered_on_time AND delivered_in_full) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT eligible_order_id), 0).
metrics.grain: Order/job grain. An order counts only if BOTH on-time and in-full are true — it's a logical AND, not two separate rates multiplied unless measured independently. Denominator = orders DUE in the window.
anti_patterns: Reporting on-time% and in-full% separately and calling either OTIF — OTIF requires both per order.
Counting shipment/line rows instead of distinct orders fans out the numerator.
Denominator = all orders instead of orders DUE/eligible in the window inflates or deflates the rate.
OTIF > 1 means the numerator population isn't a subset of the denominator — fix the join/filter.
needs_concepts: orders/jobs with due date and quantity
